title: Postmortem: 2026-02-17 Rollout latency regression — auto-rollback did not trigger due to missing/stale monitors
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate)....
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate).

During the canary step, the candidate experienced a **p99 latency regression** (tail latency increase) and a brief **5xx spike** under bursty traffic. We expected the rollout controller to auto-pause/auto-rollback based on the configured SLO monitors. No automated action occurred.

The on-call engineer manually paused the rollout after observing the degradation in Grafana. Impact was limited due to low canary percentage and quick manual mitigation, but the event revealed a critical gap: **the SLO monitor query used by the controller returned missing/stale data for the rol...

title: Rollout Incident Playbook: Auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered pause/rollback)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how on-call responders should use Redwood’s rollout auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered **pause** and/or **rollback**) during incidents, including when to trust automation vs take manual action, communication expectations, and escalation paths if auto-mitigation fails or worsens the sit...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how on-call responders should use Redwood’s rollout auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered **pause** and/or **rollback**) during incidents, including when to trust automation vs take manual action, communication expectations, and escalation paths if auto-mitigation fails or worsens the situation.

This is an incident response supplement to the deeper, step-by-step operational runbook:
- See: **Auto-rollback on SLO breach runbook** (related page)

---

## Scope
Applies to incidents involving:
- Canary rollouts, staged deployments, or A/B traffic splits for **model versions** and serving routes.
- Automated safety actions driven by **latency/error-rate/saturation** SLO monitors at a defined scope:
 - route/service, model version, region, customer tier

Out of scope:
- Multi-region failover orchestration (beyond pause/rollback)
- Quality/regression-eval-driven ...

title: Runbook: Audit Log Pipeline Incident Response (Ingestion + Export)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and ...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and correctness over perfect exactly-once semantics. Our default delivery goal is **at-least-once**; duplicates are possible and must be handled via `event_id` by consumers.

## Scope
In-scope components (names may differ slightly by region/environment):
- **Producers**: API Gateway, Control Plane (tenant/config/policy/RBAC), Console backend, Private installer/upgrade tooling
- **Audit ingest**: `audit-ingest` (HTTP/gRPC) + authn/authz for producers
- **Buffer**: Kafka topic(s) `audit.events.v1` / `audit.events.dlq` (Hosted/Dedicated)
- **Processor/normalizer**: `audit-normaliz...

title: 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative: Auto-rollback on SLO Breach for Rollouts
preview:
## Summary
This 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative hardens Redwood’s rollout safety rails by wiring rollout automation (canary + A/B + staged deployments) to SLO monitors and enabling automatic mitigation (pause and/or rollback) on verified SLO breaches.

The goal is to materially reduce customer impact and on-call load...
text:
## Summary
This 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative hardens Redwood’s rollout safety rails by wiring rollout automation (canary + A/B + staged deployments) to SLO monitors and enabling automatic mitigation (pause and/or rollback) on verified SLO breaches.

The goal is to materially reduce customer impact and on-call load from a recurring incident class: latency and error regressions introduced during rollouts (model version changes, routing policy changes, runtime config changes).

## Motivation / Why this matters
Redwood’s differentiation depends on predictable latency and reliability across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Over the last two quarters, the fastest path to customer-impacting incidents has been: 
- a rollout increases tail latency (p95/p99) or 5xx/timeout rates,
- the regression is not noticed quickly enough (or is attributed incorrectly),
- mitigation is manual a...

title: Runbook: Auto-rollback on SLO breach (triage, override, restart rollout)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s rollout controller **automatically pauses or rolls back** a rollout due to an SLO breach (latency/error-rate/saturation). It covers:
- How to confirm what happened (pause vs rollback vs suppressed)
- How to validate monitor correctness (missing, stale, ...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s rollout controller **automatically pauses or rolls back** a rollout due to an SLO breach (latency/error-rate/saturation). It covers:
- How to confirm what happened (pause vs rollback vs suppressed)
- How to validate monitor correctness (missing, stale, mis-scoped, bad labels)
- How to identify the likely culprit (model version, runtime config, region, tier)
- How to use admin overrides/suppression safely (with auditability)
- How to restart a rollout without re-triggering flapping

This is intended for **on-call SREs**, **service owners**, and **platform/console on-call**.

---

## Quick glossary
- **Rollout**: staged traffic shift from baseline → candidate (canary or A/B), possibly across regions/tiers.
- **Auto action**: controller decision taken due to SLO evaluation. One of:
 - **PAUSE**: stop progressing rollout step...

## Purpose
This runbook defines the operational procedure for enabling new inference kernels (e.g., FP8/INT8 attention + matmul paths) behind flags and rolling them out safely via perf canary before broader enablement.

The goals are:
- Detect latency/throughput regressions early (TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s) with enough statistical power.
- Minimize customer impact through cohorting, automatic rollback, and clear comms.
- Provide a repeatable, auditable checklist (what we changed, where it ran, what we observed).

## Scope
Applies to:
- Hosted API serving runtime rollouts (multi-tenant).
- Dedicated pools **only** when explicitly scheduled with Infra/SRE and customer success coordination.

Not covered:
- One-off benchmarking runs (see benchmark runner / sprint plan).
- Model catalog policy decisions (see quantization profile policy + ADR).

## Owners and on-call
- Primary operational owner (S...

## Summary
KV cache compaction reduces GPU memory fragmentation and mitigates burst-driven tail latency under mixed sequence lengths. This page documents known risks, failure modes we have observed or expect in production-like conditions, and the required mitigations/guardrails.

This is not a design doc; it is an operator- and reviewer-oriented risk register meant to be referenced during:
- rollouts (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)
- incident response / oncall
- perf regressions (p95/p99 spikes)
- correctness investigations

For operational steps, use the runbook: **Runtime KV Cache Compaction Runbook**.

---

Applies to runtime versions shipping KV compaction + tier-aware eviction behind feature flags (runtime-1.22 train).

Assumptions:
- Compaction is **budgeted** (time and/or bytes moved) and integrated with scheduler phases.
- Eviction ...

title: Postmortem: Perf Canary False-Positive Alert Storm (baseline drift + cardinality)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-12, the production performance canary generated a burst of false-positive alerts across multiple models and regions shortly after enabling warning + paging rules in the first expansion beyond the initial 2-model rollout. Alerts were high volume (warning + paging) and repeatedly reopened due to ...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-12, the production performance canary generated a burst of false-positive alerts across multiple models and regions shortly after enabling warning + paging rules in the first expansion beyond the initial 2-model rollout. Alerts were high volume (warning + paging) and repeatedly reopened due to dedupe failures, resulting in on-call interruption and a temporary release freeze request.

There was **no confirmed customer-facing latency regression** attributable to this incident. The event was an alerting/observability failure caused primarily by (1) **baseline drift + baseline key mismatch** and (2) **metric label cardinality inflation** that fragmented the canary’s time series, defeating min-sample gating and dedupe.

## Impact
### Customer impact
- **None confirmed.** Runtime and customer SLO dashboards did not show correlated sustained p95 latency increases for cu...

## Purpose
This document defines the Service Level Objectives (SLOs), Service Level Indicators (SLIs), and error budget policy for the **Cost Model API** (service name: `cost-model-api`). It covers:
- Availability and latency targets for production traffic
- A correctness/drift SLI to ensure cost outputs remain finance-usable and product-consistent
- Alerting and escalation policy

The Cost Model API is a shared dependency for **Redwood Console** cost dashboards and **Redwood Optimize** ROI/what-if projections. The SLOs below are designed to keep Console usable on hot paths while providing guardrails for cost drift that can impact customer trust and finance reporting.

## Service summary
**What it does:** Computes standardized cost primitives and breakdowns (prefill/decode, cache attribution, batch efficiency, overhead allocation) and supports what-if projections.

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to operate **KV cache compaction** and **tier-aware eviction** in the serving runtime. It is intended for **runtime on-call** and **SRE** during canaries and broad rollouts.

Compaction reduces KV allocator fragmentation under mixed sequence lengths + churn. Eviction provides a controlled pressure release to prevent opportunistic, worst-time evictions (or OOM/shedding) during bursts.

This runbook covers:
- Verifying compaction/eviction is enabled (and at what settings)
- Reading core metrics and dashboards
- Common failure modes and triage
- Adjusting budgets/thresholds safely
- Emergency kill-switch and rollback steps

## Scope / systems
**Applies to:**
- Runtime version: **runtime-1.22+** (feature-flagged)
- Deployments: Hosted API pools, Dedicated GPU pools, and Private (where enabled)

**Core components involved:**
- KV arena allocator (pages...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **primary ownership mapping** for each incident type/subtype in the v1 taxonomy, plus **remediation SLAs** (time-to-mitigate and time-to-fix expectations) by **severity** and **customer tier** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private).

The intent is to:
- Make ownership **unambiguous** during live incidents (one accountable team per incident).
- Enable consistent paging/escalation and reporting by type/owner.
- Set realistic but improvement-driving remediation expectations.

**Applies to:** customer-impacting incidents and high-severity internal incidents that materially threaten reliability.

---

## Definitions (used throughout)
- **Primary owner:** the single team accountable for mitigation and driving the incident to resolution (may delegate tasks but retains accountability).

- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline d...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

## When this runbook applies
You are in t...

title: Game Day Plan: Hot-Route Overload Simulation (Hosted API)
preview:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/avail...
text:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/availability behavior.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled/shed earlier with consistent, explainable 429 semantics.
- Routing/admission control responds to real capacity signals (queue depth/scheduler lag) without causing cascading failure.
- Observability, alerting, and on-call procedures are usable under pressure.

This is **not** a chaos test of the entire fleet. It is a controlled exercise with strict stop conditions.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted API (and Dedicated shared routing surfaces, where applicable) for one region at a time.
- Tier-aware rate limiting + quota ...

title: Regional Failover Automation Upgrade — Program Overview (Reliability Program 2026)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false posit...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false positives), slow to confirm true regional impairment, and hard to validate safely. During real incidents, this increases customer-visible errors, creates capacity cascades, and increases on-call cognitive load.

This initiative standardizes trigger signal hierarchy, adds deterministic policy evaluation and rollback guardrails, improves observability, and validates the end-to-end system via controlled failover tests (“game days”) for Hosted API and Dedicated.

---

## Problem statement
### Customer impact we are addressing
When a region is impaired (network reachability, elevated...

## Purpose
This runbook covers on-call response for overload events related to **Hot-route capacity protection** on Redwood Inference’s Hosted API and Dedicated tiers.

It helps you:
- Diagnose whether we are in **quota/rate limit** vs **overload admission control / shedding**.

- Take safe mitigation actions that protect enterprise SLOs while minimizing fleet-wide degradation.

Applies to the following high-traffic routes and route groups:
- `/v1/chat/completions`
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
- Any configured **protected route group** (see Dashboard + Policy config).

This runbook assumes enforcement occurs at:
- **API Gateway** (tier-aware rate limiting/quota evaluation, consistent 429 semantics)
- **Routing service** (priority routing + admission control under pressure)

title: Incident Classification Policy (incident_type + owner required)
preview:
## Purpose
This policy defines when and how incidents must be classified using the **Incident Taxonomy v1** (incident_type + subtype), who is responsible for setting/updating classification during an incident, how to handle “unknown” classifications, and the enforcement rules that prevent closing incidents without r...
text:
## Purpose
This policy defines when and how incidents must be classified using the **Incident Taxonomy v1** (incident_type + subtype), who is responsible for setting/updating classification during an incident, how to handle “unknown” classifications, and the enforcement rules that prevent closing incidents without required fields.

Classification is required so we can:
- Route mitigation work to the correct owning team quickly.
- Track incident volume and impact by type/owner.
- Measure remediation SLA compliance by severity and customer tier.
- Reduce recurring incidents through clear accountability.

## Scope
This policy applies to all incidents created via incident-bot and tracked in our incident tooling, including:
- Customer-impacting incidents (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private).
- High-severity internal incidents that threaten reliability (even if impact is not yet confirmed).

Th...

title: Postmortem: 2025-11-03 Sev1 — Fleet-wide latency cascade triggered by hot /v1/chat/completions route
preview:
## Summary
On 2025-11-03, the Hosted API experienced a Sev1 incident where a bursty, high-token-length workload on the `/v1/chat/completions` route caused a fleet-wide latency cascade across shared GPU pools. The event began as localized p99 latency elevation in `us-east` and progressed to elevated tail latency and ...
text:
## Summary
On 2025-11-03, the Hosted API experienced a Sev1 incident where a bursty, high-token-length workload on the `/v1/chat/completions` route caused a fleet-wide latency cascade across shared GPU pools. The event began as localized p99 latency elevation in `us-east` and progressed to elevated tail latency and increased 5xx rates across multiple routes (chat, embeddings, rerank) due to shared capacity and lack of effective tier-aware admission control.

The primary trigger was a single enterprise tenant workload (large prompts + high concurrency + streaming) that behaved like a noisy neighbor. Our control plane and routing stack at the time did not:
- enforce tier-aware budgets per route,
- gate best-effort traffic early when capacity is constrained,
- export/consume reliable queue depth signals to drive admission decisions,
- provide consistent overload semantics (429s) to shed ...

title: ADR-0142: Make incident taxonomy a required field in incident tooling
preview:

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Reliability Program is targeting a measurable reduction in incident frequency and MTTR. A recurring blocker has been incon...
text:
## Status
**Accepted / Implementing** (enforcement rollout in incident-bot + metrics started week of 2025-12-16; closure gating for Sev0/Sev1 started 2026-01-06).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Reliability Program is targeting a measurable reduction in incident frequency and MTTR. A recurring blocker has been inconsistent incident classification:

- Incidents are labeled inconsistently across Slack incident channels, incident-bot records, postmortems, and dashboards.
- Reporting by “service” alone does not reflect operational ownership in practice (e.g., incidents that manifest as 5xx at the API edge but are actually capacity shortages or routing policy failures).
- Freeform tags create high variance and poor aggregation, and they are not enforceable.
- Without consistent classification, we cannot reliably answer:
 - What are the top 10 incident types over the last quarter?
 - Which ...

## Summary
This page kicks off the Reliability Program initiative “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”. The goal is to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR by standardizing (1) incident classification, (2) owner accountability, and (3) remediation expectations (SLA timers) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

We are targeting a taxonomy that is:
- Easy to apply live during an incident (decision-tree friendly)
- Low-cardinality and stable (safe for metric labels and dashboards)
- Aligned with real operational control boundaries (single primary owner per type)
- Useful for reporting, prioritization, and error budget breakdowns

## Why we’re doing this
Current incident labeling is inconsistent (freeform tags, service-only naming, inconsistent ownership), which causes:
- Ambiguity during response (who owns mitigation vs who is consulted)
- Postmortems that are hard to ro...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Incident Taxonomy v1** used across incident-bot, dashboards, postmortems, and reporting. The taxonomy is designed to be:
- **Easy to apply during a live incident** (symptom-first, not root-cause-first)
- **Low-cardinality and stable** (safe as a metric label allowlist)
- **Consistent for reporting** (incident volume/impact by type and owner)

**How to use:** During the incident, select the **top-level incident_type** and **subtype** that best matches the *primary customer-visible symptom* at the time of peak impact.

If multiple things are true (e.g., capacity event causes routing fallback), choose the **primary type** based on the decision rules below and record the rest under **contributing systems** in the incident timeline/postmortem.

---

- **incident_type**: One of the top-level categories below (allowlis...

title: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (Reliability Program) — Overview
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experiences bursty, highly variable workloads (prompt-length variance, streaming, and customer traffic spikes). When capacity or dependencies saturate, overload can cascade into tail-latency blowups, elevated 5xx rates, and noisy-neighbor effects.

This program adds explicit...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experiences bursty, highly variable workloads (prompt-length variance, streaming, and customer traffic spikes). When capacity or dependencies saturate, overload can cascade into tail-latency blowups, elevated 5xx rates, and noisy-neighbor effects.

This program adds explicit overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) to keep the platform within SLOs and degrade safely when the system is saturated.

**Primary outcomes**
- Reject or degrade traffic early and predictably to prevent overload cascades.
- Provide customer-facing errors with stable semantics (status codes, error codes, retry guidance, correlation IDs).
- Enable safe degradation modes (fallback routing, reduced features, queued admission) while preserving tenant isolation.
- Reduce incident count/severity for capacity-related and dependency-related failure modes.
- Improve...

title: Hosted API SLO: Overload Exceptions and Error Budget Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how *overload-induced* failures are categorized for Hosted API SLO measurement, how they impact error budgets, and how we communicate the impact (internally and externally). It aligns SRE, Product, Support, and Engineering on what “counts” vs what is classified separately.

This policy a...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how *overload-induced* failures are categorized for Hosted API SLO measurement, how they impact error budgets, and how we communicate the impact (internally and externally). It aligns SRE, Product, Support, and Engineering on what “counts” vs what is classified separately.

This policy applies to the **Hosted API** (multi-tenant) request path, including gateway admission control, Smart Routing fallback, and serving runtime backpressure.

---

## Summary (what counts)
**Overload errors are user-visible failures and count against the Hosted API availability SLO for paid Hosted tiers.**

We additionally:
- Classify overload separately from generic 5xx to improve reporting and to ensure controlled degradation is preferred over timeouts.
- Track an internal “overload share” guardrail to prevent normalizing load shedding as steady-state behavior.
- Treat *custom...

title: KMS Outage Game Day — Plan + Results (2025 Q4)
preview:
## Summary
This game day exercised Redwood’s KMS dependency under controlled failure conditions (hard outage + throttling + permission misconfig) to validate:
- KMS client wrapper behavior (timeouts, retries, jitter, error taxonomy)
- Circuit breaker + degrade-mode policy to prevent retry storms / cascading failures...
text:
## Summary
This game day exercised Redwood’s KMS dependency under controlled failure conditions (hard outage + throttling + permission misconfig) to validate:
- KMS client wrapper behavior (timeouts, retries, jitter, error taxonomy)
- Circuit breaker + degrade-mode policy to prevent retry storms / cascading failures
- Observability (dashboards + alerts + traces) and on-call runbook readiness
- Customer-facing blast radius assumptions for Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments

We ran the scenarios in staging and a dedicated canary slice (internal traffic only). We did **not** run fault injection in production.

**High-level outcome:** Core availability targets were mostly met for new requests, but we observed elevated tail latency during partial KMS degradation and two operator-experience gaps (noisy alerts + unclear next steps for AccessDenied). Follow-up work was created and is tracke...

title: Runbook: Status Page Updates (Component-level)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to post and maintain **customer-facing** status page updates during incidents at Redwood Inference. It is optimized for:
- **Component-level accuracy** (product surface × region × capability)
- **Fast time-to-first-update (TTFU)** with consistent language

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to post and maintain **customer-facing** status page updates during incidents at Redwood Inference. It is optimized for:
- **Component-level accuracy** (product surface × region × capability)
- **Fast time-to-first-update (TTFU)** with consistent language
- **Verification and anti-flap behavior** (avoid noisy toggling)
- Clear coordination across **#incidents** and **#support**

This runbook applies to **Hosted API**, **Console**, and **Dedicated control-plane** customer-visible components. For **Private** deployments, see “Dedicated/Private guidance” below.

---

## Roles (who does what)
See: *Incident Comms Roles and Responsibilities* for full definitions.

- **Incident Commander (IC):** owns incident execution and severity, approves scope/impact statements.
- **Comms Lead:** owns status page updates and external-facing wording; ensures TTFU tar...

h1. Summary
This page defines the *roles, responsibilities, and approval rules* for customer-facing communications during incidents at Redwood Inference. It standardizes:
* Who owns public updates (Statuspage + customer-facing statements)
* When updates are required and how often they should happen
* How to coordinate with Support/CS so customer responses are consistent
* What content is allowed vs. prohibited (security/legal/customer sensitivity)

This process supports the “Status page transparency upgrades” initiative and is designed to reduce on-call load through templates + automation while improving time-to-first-update (TTFU) and accuracy.

h1. Scope

* Public Statuspage updates for Hosted API, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private signals

* Approval rules for high-severity incid...

It also provides guidance for handling common mismatches (public impact without SLO burn, or SLO ...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines how **public status page component states** should relate to Redwood Inference’s **internal SLOs and error budgets** across deployment modes (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**). It also provides guidance for handling common mismatches (public impact without SLO burn, or SLO burn without clear customer impact).

This is part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative and is intended for:
- SRE on-call and Incident Commanders (IC)
- Comms Leads / customer-facing incident writers
- Observability and service owners defining SLOs and alerts

## Non-goals
- Defining contractual SLAs or guaranteeing customer outcomes. (Public status is **not** an SLA statement.)
- Exhaustively listing every internal SLO. This doc focuses on the alignment patterns and the minimum required signal coverage per public component.

## Key principle: Status Page ≠ ...

title: Game Day Plan: Hosted Overload Protection (Circuit Breakers + Load Shedding)
preview:
## Summary
This game day validates Redwood Hosted API overload protection behaviors end-to-end: gateway admission control, policy evaluation, smart routing fallback, and serving runtime backpressure. The focus is safe degradation under stress (reject early, stable error semantics, avoid retry storms) while protectin...
text:
## Summary
This game day validates Redwood Hosted API overload protection behaviors end-to-end: gateway admission control, policy evaluation, smart routing fallback, and serving runtime backpressure. The focus is safe degradation under stress (reject early, stable error semantics, avoid retry storms) while protecting tenant isolation and keeping p95/p99 latency within guardrails.

## Objectives
1) Confirm overload protection prevents overload cascades (tail latency + timeouts) during bursty demand.
2) Validate customer-facing error semantics (HTTP mapping, error codes, Retry-After, correlation IDs).
3) Verify dependency brownouts do not amplify into platform-wide incidents (circuit breaker trip + fast fail).
4) Validate regional capacity dips are handled predictably (shed + optional fallback routing) without loops.
5) Exercise oncall operational muscle: detection, triage, mitigations,...

title: Postmortem: 2025-11-07 US-East Streaming Degradation
preview:
## Summary
On 2025-11-07, customers using **streaming responses** in **US-East** experienced elevated disconnects and intermittent upstream errors. Non-streaming requests were largely unaffected.

This incident exposed a major gap in our customer-facing communication: the public status page component coverage was to...
text:
## Summary
On 2025-11-07, customers using **streaming responses** in **US-East** experienced elevated disconnects and intermittent upstream errors. Non-streaming requests were largely unaffected.

This incident exposed a major gap in our customer-facing communication: the public status page component coverage was too coarse ("Hosted API" only), which made it difficult to accurately reflect a streaming-only regional degradation. The resulting delay and ambiguity in external updates directly contributed to customer confusion and escalations.

**Severity:** Sev1

## Customer impact
**Who was affected**
- Customers using streaming (SSE) for Chat Completions via the Hosted API in **us-east-1**.
- Impact was most visible for long-lived streams (longer generations / higher token counts).

**What customers experienced**
- Increased streaming disconnects (client-visible mid-stream termination)...

## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage and remediate failures in the **Provision-to-Ready** automation for GPU nodes (Dedicated and shared capacity). It focuses on:
- Quickly identifying *where* a node is stuck (Provisioning vs Validating)
- Finding authoritative failure reasons (Fleet Readiness Service + validation artifacts)
- Applying safe remediation steps (retry validation, drain, quarantine, reimage)
- Escalation paths and when to stop the line

## Audience
- Infra/Fleet on-call
- SRE on-call (paging + alert tuning)
- Platform Control Plane on-call (readiness state/API issues)

## Definitions (canonical)
Readiness state machine (see ADR-017):
- **Allocated**: capacity allocated, node not yet being processed by orchestrator
- **Provisioning**: instance boot + bootstrap + cluster join
- **Validating**: pre/post-flight checks executing and reporting results
- **Ready**: pa...

text:
## Purpose
Validate that the provision-to-ready automation can safely detect common bring-up failures, prevent unsafe nodes from entering serving pools, and provide actionable diagnostics for operators.

This game day intentionally introduces controlled failures for:
- Golden image mismatch
- Driver install failure / driver-toolkit parity drift
- Kubelet join failure
- NCCL smoke test failure

We will evaluate:
- Correct readiness state transitions (Allocated → Provisioning → Validating → Ready | Quarantined)
- Quality of failure reasons + artifacts (logs pointers, step durations)
- Retry/backoff behavior and quarantine policy
- Operational response: triage, remediation, and recovery time

## Scope
In scope:
- Dedicated pools only (staged rollout configuration)
- One region + one GPU type (recommended: us-east / A100) unless explicitly approved for multi-region
- Control-plane readine...

The goals are:
- Restore customer latency/SLOs quickly while respecting safety invariants (never exceed max; never scale below...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook helps on-call engineers diagnose and mitigate Dedicated autoscaling issues after the introduction of **customer min/max bounds** and optional **predictive scaling**.

The goals are:
- Restore customer latency/SLOs quickly while respecting safety invariants (never exceed max; never scale below min).
- Provide deterministic, auditable actions (document config changes and mitigations).
- Reduce escalation ping-pong by giving clear ownership boundaries.

## Scope
Applies to **Redwood Dedicated** deployments using the Dedicated autoscaler (fleet-managed GPU pools).

In-scope symptoms:
- Stuck scale-up or stuck scale-down
- Sustained queueing / saturation when at **max bound**
- **Min bound unmet** (region shortage / provisioning failures)
- Prediction pipeline outages or stale forecasts
- Oscillation / jitter related to predictive inputs
- Config churn / throttling ...

## Purpose
This runbook describes how to run (or validate) Prometheus and Grafana in **customer Kubernetes clusters** for **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). It focuses on the minimum supported needs for the “Private observability minimum pack”:

- Practical sizing guidance (CPU/memory/storage)

 - scrape failures
 - missing targets/service discovery issues

 - slow or empty dashboards

This document is written for Infra/SRE and Solutions Engineering supporting customer installs.

## Scope and support boundaries

1) **Bundled stack (recommended for “turnkey supported minimum”)**

title: ADR-047: Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds — Precedence and Safety Invariants
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **authoritative semantics** for customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (min/max) for **Redwood Dedicated** deployments, including:
- Precedence between customer bounds and platform defaults
- Conflict resolution between **predictive** and **reactive** scaling signals
- Safety invari...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **authoritative semantics** for customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (min/max) for **Redwood Dedicated** deployments, including:
- Precedence between customer bounds and platform defaults
- Conflict resolution between **predictive** and **reactive** scaling signals
- Safety invariants for scale-down (and scale-up) behavior
- Deterministic evaluation requirements for auditability

The goal is to provide enterprise-grade, contract-aligned guardrails that are **hard enforced**, **observable**, and **operationally safe**.

---

## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature flags; required for GA).

---

## Context
Redwood Dedicated historically relies on platform-driven autoscaling (reactive metrics + operator overrides). Enterprise customers require:
- A **hard maximum** to prevent unexpected spend and enforce contractual ceilings.
- A **hard mini...

title: Runbook: Burst Pool Overload Mitigation (Dedicated Burst Capacity)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, diagnose, mitigate, and communicate during **Burst Pool Overload** events for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

A burst pool overload is any situation where shared Redwood-managed burst pools (per region + hardware class) become saturated or unstable, and/or where **non-...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, diagnose, mitigate, and communicate during **Burst Pool Overload** events for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

A burst pool overload is any situation where shared Redwood-managed burst pools (per region + hardware class) become saturated or unstable, and/or where **non-burst tenant SLOs are threatened** by burst activity. The runbook prioritizes:
1) Protecting baseline Dedicated SLOs for all tenants (including non-burst tenants)
2) Restoring stable operation of the burst pool
3) Minimizing customer-impact and providing clear customer-facing explanations

## Scope
**In scope:** overload/saturation of shared burst pools, admission controller instability, telemetry lag causing unfair denials, routing amplification, global fleet safety cap triggers.

**Out of scope:** GPU host-level failures unrelated to burst pools (see Fleet hardware runboo...

2) **Traffic split mismatch** (observed baseline/canary traffic does not match the configur...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers two common incident modes for **Hosted canary rollouts**:

1) **Rollout is stuck** (step not progressing, state machine not advancing, approvals not unblocking, or auto-stop evaluation hanging).
2) **Traffic split mismatch** (observed baseline/canary traffic does not match the configured percentage, or behaves differently by region).

It provides a fast triage path, diagnostic checks, safe mitigations, rollback steps, and escalation guidance.

---

## Scope / systems
**Applies to:** Hosted routes using percentage-based canary split.

**Core components involved:**
- **Console** (customer UI + orchestrator client)
- **Rollout Orchestrator** (control-plane service; rollout state machine)
- **Smart Routing / Data Plane** (enforces split; deterministic cohorting)
- **Policy Engine** (guardrails, approvals, soak-time rules)
- **Telemetry Pipeline** (cohort tag...

## Purpose
This runbook covers alerts and common failure modes that occur during **Redwood Private** upgrades (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). It provides:
- Fast triage steps to distinguish expected upgrade noise vs. actionable incidents.
- Mitigation playbooks for the most common alert categories (error rate spikes, DB migration issues, control-plane connectivity, capacity/resource pressure, and certificate/identity problems).
- Clear escalation and rollback decision points.

This runbook is intended for **SRE on-call** and release captain support during planned upgrade windows.

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Alerts triggered during an upgrade window for a single Private environment.
- Installer-driven upgrades (redwood-private-installer), Helm upgrades, control-plane/data-plane rollouts.
- Post-upgrade verification and “upgrade stuck” scenarios.

**Out of scope**
- Initial install fail...

It includes common failure modes, triage steps, mitigations (pause/resume exports, credential rotation, DLQ handling), and customer...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers on-call response for **audit-log-exporter** delivery issues impacting the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs intake, and S3 delivery). It includes common failure modes, triage steps, mitigations (pause/resume exports, credential rotation, DLQ handling), and customer communications guidance.

This integration is **at-least-once** delivery. Downstream consumers must deduplicate using `event_id` (see ADR).

## Scope
**In scope:** Hosted and Dedicated environments using the managed exporter service.

**Out of scope for beta:** Private/on-prem deployments (documented follow-on).

## Definitions
- **Exporter**: `audit-log-exporter` service responsible for reading audit events and delivering to customer destinations.
- **Destination**: One of Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, or S3.
- **Lag**: Time between audit event creation (`event_ts`) and succe...

The plan covers:
- Synthetic bursts (single tenant and multi-tenant)

text:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **Dedicated Burst Capacity** guardrails protect non-bursting tenants and platform SLOs under stress conditions that are hard to reproduce organically.

The plan covers:
- Synthetic bursts (single tenant and multi-tenant)
- Admission control under pool saturation
- Fairness and anti-starvation behavior
- Induced **telemetry lag** and stale capacity signals
- Routing failures (timeouts, partial dependency outages)
- Rollback drills (feature flags, global circuit breaker, per-region/hardware allow-list)

Success criteria and rollback triggers are explicitly defined so on-call can make fast decisions.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Burst pool(s) in a single region/hardware class (primary), and a secondary region/hardware class (sanity)
- Admission controller behavior and denial reason codes
- Routing integration (admission decision consumption, backp...

title: Incident Bot v2 Triage Playbook (First 5 Minutes)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how on-call responders use Incident Bot v2 diagnostics in the first 5 minutes of an incident. The goal is to reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation (MTTR) by quickly answering three questions:
1) Did we just deploy something?
2) Did config/routing/policy change?

text:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how on-call responders use Incident Bot v2 diagnostics in the first 5 minutes of an incident. The goal is to reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation (MTTR) by quickly answering three questions:
1) Did we just deploy something?
2) Did config/routing/policy change?
3) Are we saturated (GPU/queue/KV cache/capacity) and should we degrade?

Incident Bot v2 posts a set of Slack “context cards” into #incidents and #eng-oncall threads. Cards are designed to be safe-by-default (tenant-safe, redacted), bounded-cost (rate limited + cached), and resilient (degraded mode under partial outages).

---

## When to use this
Use this playbook for:
- Any Sev0/Sev1, and any Sev2 where symptoms suggest deploy regression, config change, or regional saturation.
- Common symptom patterns: 503 spikes, p99 latency regression, streaming disconnects/timeouts, sudden thr...

title: Runbook: Recent deploys + config diff debugging (Incident Bot v2)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook explains how to interpret the Incident Bot v2 **Recent deploys** and **Config diff** context cards during the first 5–15 minutes of an incident. It also covers common pitfalls (staged rollouts, partial propagation, config layering) and manual fallback commands when bot context is missing or s...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook explains how to interpret the Incident Bot v2 **Recent deploys** and **Config diff** context cards during the first 5–15 minutes of an incident. It also covers common pitfalls (staged rollouts, partial propagation, config layering) and manual fallback commands when bot context is missing or suspicious.

**Use this when:**
- Errors/latency spike shortly after a deploy or config push
- Symptoms vary by region, pool, or tenant
- The bot shows a config diff you don’t fully trust (redaction, partial propagation, multiple layers)

**Do not use this for:**
- GPU saturation / queueing root-cause analysis (use the GPU saturation triage runbook)
- End-to-end incident process (use the incident process playbook)

---

## Quick workflow (first 5 minutes)
1) Identify **scope**: service/route, region, deployment mode (Hosted / Dedicated / Private), and at least one affected t...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to operate **Dedicated staged rollouts** for **reserved-capacity pools** ("Dedicated pools"). It covers:
- Creating and validating a DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan
- Running and promoting stages
- Interpreting gate failures (including missing/lagging telemetry)
- Manual override procedures (hold / force-promote / abort)
- Emergency rollback steps and what to do during incidents

Staged rollouts are used to safely deploy changes to a Dedicated pool, including:
- Model version upgrades (weights/tokenizer)
- Serving runtime build changes
- Routing policy changes (e.g., fallback behavior)
- Performance/caching/batching configuration changes

## Scope and non-goals
**In scope:** operational procedures and safety controls for rollouts that partition a Dedicated pool into stage segments (traffic or replica partitions) with measurable gates.

**Not a general ex...

title: Reliability Review: Repeated Tensor-Parallel (TP) Timeouts and Stall Events
preview:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents present...
text:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents presented as different customer-visible symptoms (streaming timeouts, elevated p99 latency, occasional 5xx/504 spikes), investigation strongly suggests a shared class of TP stall modes:
- Rank-level stalls inside or around NCCL collectives (silent hangs rather than explicit NCCL errors)
- Scheduler ordering / fairness issues that can starve collectives under mixed prefill/decode
- Edge cases where cancellation and/or KV cache compaction under memory pressure creates rank divergence

This doc consolidates observed signals, contributing factors, and SRE-driven reliability actions (a...

title: TP stall triage playbook (tensor-parallel hangs/timeouts)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook covers step-by-step triage for **tensor-parallel (TP) stalls** (a.k.a. hangs/timeouts) in the serving runtime. It is written for on-call SRE + runtime engineers to quickly answer:
- Is the stall **compute**, **NCCL/collectives**, or **scheduler/starvation**?
- Which replicas/ranks are stuck,...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook covers step-by-step triage for **tensor-parallel (TP) stalls** (a.k.a. hangs/timeouts) in the serving runtime. It is written for on-call SRE + runtime engineers to quickly answer:
- Is the stall **compute**, **NCCL/collectives**, or **scheduler/starvation**?
- Which replicas/ranks are stuck, and is it isolated or systemic?
- What data should we capture (metrics/logs/traces/NCCL debug) without making the incident worse?

This is **triage + profiling**. For mitigation actions (drain/replace, concurrency caps, feature flags), use the runbook: *Runtime TP hang mitigation*.

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “TP stall”)
A TP stall is a condition where a multi-GPU replica stops making forward progress for a request or decode loop. Typical external symptoms:
- Streaming responses stop mid-stream (client sees partial tokens then timeout).
- Request p99 and timeout ...

title: Postmortem: 2025-02-07 Hosted API elevated 503s due to deploy regression
preview:
## Summary
On 2025-02-07, Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experienced elevated HTTP 503 responses following a production deploy of the **inference-router** service (request admission + routing layer). A regression in the new release increased the probability of transient upstream timeout handling being converted into...
text:
## Summary
On 2025-02-07, Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experienced elevated HTTP 503 responses following a production deploy of the **inference-router** service (request admission + routing layer). A regression in the new release increased the probability of transient upstream timeout handling being converted into immediate 503s under certain concurrency patterns.

The incident was resolved by halting the rollout and rolling back to the prior stable version. The incident highlighted a major operational gap: responders did not have **reliable “recent deploy context” in the first five minutes**, which materially increased time-to-triage.

This postmortem is one of the primary justifications for the **Incident bot + diagnostics improvements (Incident Bot v2)** initiative.

---

## Customer impact
**Customer-visible symptoms**
- Elevated 503 responses from Hosted API endpoints (chat/com...

title: Private DR Game Day Plan (Backup/Restore Toolkit Rehearsal)
preview:
## Summary
This game day validates Redwood Private Day-2 operations for backup/restore and disaster recovery (DR) in a controlled, non-production environment. The rehearsal is designed to be repeatable by SRE/Solutions teams and to produce concrete follow-up actions (runbook fixes, tooling gaps, alert tuning, and re...
text:
## Summary
This game day validates Redwood Private Day-2 operations for backup/restore and disaster recovery (DR) in a controlled, non-production environment. The rehearsal is designed to be repeatable by SRE/Solutions teams and to produce concrete follow-up actions (runbook fixes, tooling gaps, alert tuning, and release readiness).

**Primary outcome:** demonstrate a deterministic restore of Redwood Private control-plane state into a clean Kubernetes cluster using the supported toolkit and runbooks, then pass post-restore validation (health + functional smoke tests) within agreed RTO.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1. Validate **end-to-end control-plane restore** from an encrypted backup artifact using the supported `restore apply` workflow and documented bootstrap order.
2. Validate **RPO/RTO expectations** for the chosen archetype (VPC single-region reference environment) a...
